1: Eliphaz opens the second dialogue cycle (Job 15–21). The first cycle (Job 4–14) saw the Friends argue from general retribution principles and Job respond with increasingly direct challenges to God. The second cycle shifts: the Friends become more personal and accusatory, and their speeches become less philosophical and more polemical. Eliphaz, who opened the first cycle with gentleness (Job 4:2: “can we hold back our words?”), now opens with pointed attack. The cycle structure — three rounds of speeches with Job responding to each — is one of the most architecturally sophisticated features of the book.
2: “Should a wise man answer with empty words, filling himself with the hot east wind?” Eliphaz attacks Job’s wisdom credentials immediately. Rûaḥ qāḏîm (hot east wind) is the desert sirocco — scorching, desiccating, destructive. In the ancient Near East, the east wind was associated with destruction (Genesis 41:6,23 — the east wind withered the good grain in Pharaoh’s dream; Ezekiel 17:10; Hosea 13:15). To be “full of the east wind” is to be full of hot, damaging, empty talk. Eliphaz is reversing Job’s wisdom-credentials: Job claimed to have the wisdom the Friends lack (Job 12:3; Job 13:2); Eliphaz now claims Job speaks hot wind, not wisdom.
3: “Should he argue with worthless words, with speeches that accomplish nothing?” The two criteria Eliphaz applies are ḏāḇār lōʾ-yiskōn (words that profit nothing) and millîm lōʾ-yôʿîl (speeches that do not benefit). These are wisdom-pragmatic criteria: useful speech versus useless speech. In the wisdom tradition, words that do not benefit the hearer and speaker are categorically worthless (Proverbs 10:19; Job 15:2; Job 18:21). Eliphaz is applying the wisdom tradition’s own criteria against Job’s complaint-speech. The irony: wisdom speech is being used to silence wisdom-speech.
4: “You are actually undermining the fear of God and hindering prayer before him.” This is Eliphaz’s most serious theological charge: Job’s speeches threaten the foundational structures of religion. Yirʾat ʾĕlôah (fear of God) is the book’s programmatic term — Job is described as fearing God in Job 1:1,8,9; Job 2:3. To undermine the fear of God is to attack the foundation of wisdom (Job 28:28) and covenant-fidelity. Śîaḥ (prayer/meditation) before God — Eliphaz accuses Job of making prayer itself seem pointless. This charge accurately describes the social danger of Job’s position even if it misrepresents Job’s intent.
5: “Your mouth reveals your own guilt; you have adopted the tongue of the cunning.” ʿᵃwōneḵā (your guilt/iniquity) is revealed by the mouth itself — the speech-as-evidence principle. Lᵉšôn ʿᵃrûmîm (the tongue of the cunning/clever) — the word ʿārûm is the same used for the serpent in Genesis 3:1. By accusing Job of serpentine cleverness, Eliphaz is casting him as a tempter who seduces others away from God. This is the escalation from Job 4–5: there Eliphaz merely suggested Job had some hidden fault; now he accuses Job of actively subverting faith.
6: “Your own mouth condemns you, not I; your very lips bear witness against you.” Eliphaz steps back to appear impartial: he is not the prosecutor but the observer of Job’s self-incrimination. This is the ancient legal principle that spontaneous speech could be used as evidence (cf. Proverbs 12:13: “by the transgression of his lips an evil man is ensnared”). In Eliphaz’s framework, Job’s complaints — especially his direct challenges to God — are the evidence of the guilt that originally produced the suffering. The circular logic here is what Job will spend the rest of the dialogue exposing.
7: “Are you the first man who was ever born? Were you shaped before the hills?” Eliphaz invokes the rîʾšôn ʾāḏām (first/primordial man) tradition — the mythological figure of a pre-existent human who witnessed creation. This figure appears in Ezekiel’s oracle against the prince of Tyre (Ezekiel 28:12–14: “you were the model of perfection, full of wisdom... you were in Eden, the garden of God”) and has parallels in Ugaritic texts describing El and Baal as the eldest of the primordial figures. The hills (gᵉḇāʿôṯ) predate human civilization in biblical cosmology (Proverbs 8:25). To be born before the hills would mean predating creation itself — wisdom’s own territory (Proverbs 8:22–25).
8: “Have you been present in God’s secret council? Do you think wisdom belongs to you alone?” The sôḏ (secret council, intimate assembly) of God is a significant concept in OT prophecy. Jeremiah 23:18,22 uses it as the criterion distinguishing true from false prophets: “who has stood in the council of the LORD?” Amos 3:7 notes that God reveals his plans to his servants the prophets. The divine council appears in Job’s prologue (Job 1:6–12; Job 2:1–6) and in 1 Kings 22:19–23 (Micaiah’s vision). Eliphaz is mockingly suggesting Job claims council-access — wisdom as his personal possession. The irony: Job’s suffering was in fact decided in the divine council, though Job does not know this.
9: “What do you know that we do not? What insight have you that we lack?” The epistemological challenge — mah-yadaʿtā (what do you know?) — mirrors Job’s own challenge in Job 12:3 (“I have understanding as well as you; I am not inferior to you”). Eliphaz turns Job’s epistemological confidence back against him. In the wisdom tradition, knowledge claims are to be tested against the tradition of the elders (v10), personal experience, and the instruction of the community. Eliphaz is marshaling all three against Job.
10: “Among us are both the gray-haired and the very aged, men far older than your father.” The Friends’ authority claim: tradition mediated through age. In ancient Near Eastern wisdom, śêḇāh (gray hair) and advanced age were marks of authentic wisdom-transmission. The Instruction of Amenemope, the Instructions of Ptahhotep, and Mesopotamian wisdom texts all appeal to the sage’s great age as credential. Job’s father is presumably Eliphaz’s contemporary; by claiming superiority over Job’s father’s generation, Eliphaz claims the highest tier of traditional authority. Job will later be vindicated over these very elders (Job 42:7–9).
11: “Are God’s consolations not enough for you — the gentle word he has offered you?” Tanḥûmôṯ ʾēl (consolations of God) — Eliphaz refers to his own counsel as divine consolation. The claim is extraordinary: the Friends are offering God’s own comfort through their speeches. Wᵉḏāḇār laʾaṭ ʿimmāḵ (the gentle word with you) — the Friends’ initial approach was indeed gentle (Job 2:13: seven days of silence). But Job’s rejection of their comfort has escalated the confrontation. Eliphaz is partly right that the comfort was genuine in intent; what he misses is that it was theologically inadequate to Job’s actual situation.
12: “Why does your heart carry you away? Why do your eyes flare?” Mah-yiqqāḥᵃḵā libbᵉḵā (what carries away/seduces your heart?) — the heart as the seat of volition and moral judgment in ANE psychology. When the heart is “carried away,” it has lost its grounding in received wisdom and sound judgment. The eye-flash (wᵉmah-yirzemûn ʿênêḵā) may refer to the glittering look of anger or spiritual arrogance — the outward sign of an inner state that has become unmoored from the tradition. Eliphaz diagnoses Job’s problem as psychological/spiritual disorientation rather than theological error.
13: “That you turn your spirit against God and pour out such words from your mouth?” Tāšêḇ ʾel-ʾēl rûḥeḵā (you turn your spirit against God) — the direct accusation of spiritual antagonism toward God. In the wisdom tradition, the spirit (rûaḥ) aligned with God is the source of life and wisdom; a spirit turned against God is dangerous and self-destructive. Wᵉḥôṣēʾṯā mippîḵā millîn (and you bring out such words from your mouth) — the progression is clear: the turned spirit produces the dangerous words. Eliphaz’s diagnosis frames Job’s theological challenge as spiritual rebellion rather than honest protest.
14: “What is a mortal man, that he could be clean? Can one born of woman be righteous?” This verse reprises Eliphaz’s first-cycle assertion (Job 4:17: “Can a mortal be more righteous than God? Can even a strong man be more pure than his Maker?”) but with different framing: not righteousness relative to God but the inherent impurity of being yᵉlûḏ ʾiššāh (born of woman). The phrase “born of woman” is a standard idiom for mortal human frailty (Job 14:1; Psalms 51:5; Galatians 4:4). Eliphaz is arguing from creatureliness: the category “creature born of woman” precludes the category “righteous before God.”
15: “God does not even trust his holy ones; the heavens themselves are not clean in his sight.” The “holy ones” (qᵉḏōšāyw) are the members of the divine council — the heavenly beings who surround God’s throne (Job 1:6: “the sons of God”; Psalms 82:1; Daniel 4:13). If even these are not trusted by God, how much less a human? This argument escalates the scale: from the Friend’s first-cycle point (Job 4:18: even angels are found wanting) to now making the heavens themselves impure. The cosmological scale of divine holiness makes human righteousness-claims look absurd. Qumran community texts later developed this heavenly-purity theology extensively.
16: “How much more abominable and vile is man, who laps up wickedness like water!” The qal wāḥōmer (light-and-heavy) argument from v15: if heavenly beings are not trusted, ʾap kî (how much more) the creature who drinks iniquity like water. Niteʿāḇ wᵉneʾᵃlāḥ (abominable and vile) — the strongest words for moral corruption, used elsewhere in contexts of idolatry and sexual sin (Deuteronomy 24:4; Psalms 14:1). Eliphaz’s rhetoric has intensified enormously from his gentle opening in Job 4:2–6. The second cycle reveals how the Friends’ theodicy, under pressure, moves toward denunciation.
17: “I will show you — listen to me.” The instruction formula (šimʿāh-lî, listen to me) transitions Eliphaz from accusation to teaching. What follows (vv18–35) is the fate-of-the-wicked poem that occupies the bulk of Job 15. By prefacing it with personal testimony (“what I have seen”) and traditional authority (“what wise men have declared,” v18), Eliphaz frames his description of the wicked as both empirically and traditionally validated. The implicit application to Job makes this the most extensive indirect accusation in the book.
18: “What wise men have declared, passed down from their fathers without concealment.” The appeal to unbroken tradition (miʾᵃḇôṯām lōʾ-kiḥēḏû, from their fathers without concealment) is the classic wisdom-authority claim: accumulated experiential knowledge, faithfully transmitted across generations. This contrasts with Job’s implied claim to direct insight that overrides tradition. The wisdom literature of Egypt and Mesopotamia similarly invokes the chain of transmission: Ptahhotep opens with the wisdom of ancestors; Amenemope addresses his son as recipient of the tradition. Eliphaz positions himself within this authoritative stream and Job outside it.
19: “To those whose land was their own alone, before foreigners came among them.” This parenthesis may assert the purity of the tradition: it originated in a land uncorrupted by foreign influence. Some interpreters see it as a reference to pre-Israelite patriarchal tradition — the Edomite wisdom tradition, given that Eliphaz is a Temanite from Edom, a region renowned for wisdom (Jeremiah 49:7; Obad 8). The tradition Eliphaz claims is ethnic and territorial, not merely professional. This grounds his authority claim in deep indigenous wisdom rather than mere learned craft.
20: “The wicked man twists in anguish all his days; the years allotted to the tyrant are numbered.” The fate-of-the-wicked poem begins. Mithôlēl (twisting in anguish, writhing) describes persistent torment — not a sudden catastrophe but a lifelong condition of inner suffering. ʿārîṣ (tyrant, ruthless man) — the word used for oppressors and violent rulers throughout the prophets (Isaiah 13:11; Job 25:3–5; Psalms 37:35). Eliphaz’s portrait begins with existential anguish: the wicked man is not at peace even in apparent prosperity. This is psychologically astute even if theologically misapplied to Job.
21: “A sound of terror is always in his ears; in his moments of peace the destroyer strikes him.” Qôl-pᵉḥāḏîm bᵉʾoznāyw (a sound of terrors in his ears) — perpetual anxiety as the inner life of the wicked. Bišlômāh (in his peace/security) the destroyer comes — the word šālôm (peace, wholeness) normally denotes the condition of the righteous; for the wicked, even apparent peace contains the seed of terror. Šōḏēḏ (destroyer) — a term used for the divine agent of destruction (Jeremiah 48:8; Job 51:56). The wicked man can never enjoy the peace that is the righteous man’s inheritance.
22: “He has no hope of escaping from the darkness; he is marked for the sword.” Lōʾ-yaʾᵃmên šûḇ (he does not trust/believe he can return) from the darkness — a permanent condition of hopelessness. The darkness that envelopes Job (Job 10:21–22) is here attributed to the wicked. This is the first point of contact between Eliphaz’s description of the wicked and Job’s description of his own condition — a contact that makes the implicit application to Job increasingly clear as the poem develops. Ṣāpûn hûʾ liḥāreḇ (he is appointed for the sword) — his fate is sealed.
23: “He wanders in search of food, crying, ‘Where is it?’ He knows the day of darkness is ready and near.” The wandering hunger of the wicked contrasts with the settled prosperity of the righteous. Nāḏeḏ hûʾ lāleḥem ʾayyēh (he wanders for bread — where?) — the exile-and-hunger motif that in the prophets marks divine judgment (Lamentations 1:11; Job 4:9). The awareness of impending darkness is the psychological torment: not only does the day of darkness come, but the wicked person knows it is near. This consciousness of doom-without-escape is the distinctive signature of Eliphaz’s wicked man.
24: “Distress and anguish terrorize him; they overpower him like a king advancing to battle.” Ṣar ûmᵉṣûqāh (distress and anguish) — the classic paired terms of covenant curse (Deuteronomy 28:53,55,57; Isaiah 8:22; Zephaniah 1:15). The simile “like a king advancing to battle” (kᵉmelek ʿāṯîḏ lᵉkîḏôr) — the wicked person’s internal suffering overwhelms him like an invading army. This is the ancient Near Eastern war-metaphor for psychological/spiritual crisis. The comparison inverts normal power: the “great man” (by worldly measures) is overwhelmed by forces he cannot fight.
25: “For he has stretched out his hand against God and defied the Almighty.” The explicit sin of the wicked man: hand-stretching against God (nāṭāh ʿal-ʾēl yāḏô) and defiance (ʾel-šaddai yitgabbār). This is Eliphaz’s implicit charge against Job: Job’s speeches constitute exactly this — stretching the hand against God, acting with defiance against the Almighty. The escalation from the first-cycle speech (Job 4:7–8) is evident: then Eliphaz hinted; now he charges. The rhetoric of the wicked man is designed to describe Job without naming him.
26: “Charging at him neck-forward, pressing against the thick boss of his shield.” The combat imagery intensifies: the wicked man charges God like a warrior (yārûṣ ʾēlāyw bᵉṣawwārô, charging at him neck-forward). Bᵉgaḇ bᵉginnôṯ māginnāyw (pressing against the thick bosses of his shields) — the bosses are the raised metal knobs on a warrior’s shield, the most armored part. Attacking the most armored part of God’s defense is the height of futile arrogance. The image participates in the OT warrior-God tradition (Exod 15; Hab 3; Psalms 24:8) but here inverted: God is the defender against the wicked aggressor.
27: “He has become fat-faced, his sides padded with flesh.” The transition from warrior imagery to prosperity imagery: the wicked man is physically fat (kāśāh pānāyw bᵉḥêlpāyw, covered his face with his flesh) and wealthy-looking. In the ancient world, physical corpulence was a sign of prosperity (Psalms 17:10; Job 73:4–7; Jeremiah 5:28). The wicked man’s outward flourishing is the context for the destruction that follows: he looks prosperous, but he is building on a foundation that will collapse. This is the paradox of Psalm 73 and Proverbs 1–9 — the temporary prosperity of the wicked.
28: “He makes his home in ruined cities, in houses no one else inhabits.” The dwelling-in-ruins detail is striking: despite apparent prosperity (v27), the wicked man inhabits condemned places. This may reflect a judgment-tradition: the wicked are those who take possession of places under divine curse (compare the destruction of Sodom — Genesis 19:24–25 — and prophetic descriptions of ruined cities as signs of divine judgment: Isaiah 13:20–22; Jeremiah 50:39–40). The wicked man’s home is already under curse even when he occupies it; his prosperity is built on condemned ground.
29: “He will not remain wealthy; his substance will not last.” The prediction of reversal: current prosperity will not endure. Lōʾ-yeʿᵃšer (he will not be wealthy), lōʾ-yāqûm ḥêlô (his wealth will not endure). This is the foundational claim of the two-ways theology: eventual reversal of apparent prosperity. The wisdom literature consistently argues this case (Psalms 37:1–2,9–10; Job 49:16–20; Proverbs 11:28). Eliphaz is applying to the wicked in general what he implies Job’s future will be if Job continues on his current path.
30: “He will not escape from the darkness; flame will wither his branches, and by the breath of God’s mouth he will be swept away.” Lōʾ-yāsûr miṇ-ḥōšeḵ (he will not turn from the darkness) — the darkness is permanent entrapment. Yibaššēš šalhaḇeṯ nîṣṣô (flame will wither his blossom/branch) — the tree imagery for human life: the wicked man’s flourishing branch is scorched. Wᵉyāsur bᵉrûaḥ pîhû (and he will be removed by the breath of God’s mouth) — the divine breath that gives life (Job 33:4) here removes the wicked man like wind blows away chaff (Psalms 1:4; Isaiah 17:13). Creation-breath becomes judgment-breath.
31: “Let him not deceive himself by trusting in what is empty, for emptiness will be his only return.” Šāwᵉ (emptiness, vanity, falsehood) as both the foundation the wicked trusts and the reward they receive — the boomerang principle of Proverbs (Proverbs 1:18: “they lie in wait for their own blood”). The verse may be a warning-instruction or a description: either “let him not deceive himself” (hortatory) or “he deceives himself” (descriptive). The ambiguity is itself meaningful: both the warning and its violation are present simultaneously in the wicked man’s situation.
32: “It will be completed before his time; his branch will not stay green.” Bᵉlōʾ-yômô (before his day/time) — premature death as divine judgment. The green branch (kappô ṯirʾᵃneh) that fails to remain green is the tree-of-life image inverted: instead of the righteous man’s flourishing (Psalms 1:3; Proverbs 11:28: “the righteous will flourish like a green leaf”), the wicked man’s branch withers prematurely. The temporal element (before his time) responds to the problem of delayed justice that Job has raised: the wicked’s prosperity is always temporary even when it appears enduring.
33: “He will shed his unripe grapes like the vine, and drop his blossoms like the olive tree.” The agricultural metaphors: fruit production that fails before maturity. Yāḥmōs kāggepen bišrô (he will violently strip off his unripe grapes) — the ḥāmas (violence, stripping) is done to his own fruit; his violence destroys his own productivity. The olive blossom (nēṣeṯ zāyiṯ) that drops before bearing fruit is a standard image of failed potential (Deuteronomy 28:40). The wicked man’s descendants and projects are the “fruit” that never matures — a generational curse.
34: “For the assembly of the godless will be desolate, and fire will consume the tents of corruption.” ʿᵃḏaṯ ḥānēp (assembly of the godless/profane) — the community rather than just the individual. The fate-of-the-wicked judgment extends to the social group: the whole assembly will be desolate (galmûḏ, barren/desolate). Bribĕ šōḥaḏ (tents of bribery/corruption) — in the ANE, bribery and corruption were the emblematic sins of unjust rulers and judges. Fire as divine judgment on corrupt settlements recalls the fate of Sodom and Gomorrah (Genesis 19:24) and the prophetic judgment oracles (Amos 1–2).
35: “They conceive trouble and give birth to evil; their inmost being produces only deceit.” The reproductive metaphor for wickedness — hārāh ʿāmāl wᵉyāled ʾāwen (conceiving trouble and giving birth to iniquity) — recalls Psalms 7:14 (“he who conceives trouble and gives birth to lies”) and Isaiah 59:4 (“they conceive trouble and give birth to evil”). The ḥarsô (their inner part/belly) that produces mirmāh (deceit) closes the poem with an inwardness claim: the wicked person’s core is generative of nothing but deception. The poem’s structural movement — from existential anguish (v20) to explicit rebellion (v25) to physical destruction (vv30–34) to moral inwardness (v35) — is deliberately comprehensive.
